cataloging_subject.label: "Three Inspections" Hematoscopy
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The coherent opening hematoscopy passage of the anonymous Middle English On Bloodletting tradition represented in CME301 by parallel Sloane 3486 and Hunter 307 versions. It is a work-part, not the complete On Bloodletting text, either complete manuscript, the sixty XML alignment units, or the modern digital encoding.

catalog_summary.title: "Three Inspections" Hematoscopy

catalog_summary.editor: M. Teresa Tavormina and Paul F. Schaffner (CME transcribers)
catalog_summary.date: Middle English version attested by the early fifteenth century; exact translation date unknown (Latin Chirurgia magna composed 1363)
catalog_summary.region: England; Hunter 307's scribal language is Midland, probably Huntingdonshire, but the translation's place is unresolved

assertion.notes: An editorial discovery title in the source header and text head, not a manuscript title.

date_statements.display: Translated into Middle English no later than the early fifteenth-century Hunter 307 attestation; exact date unknown

assertion.notes: The broad normalized terminus avoids assigning the translation a false single year or assuming that all surviving copies reflect one translation event.

date_statements.display: At least one attestation, Hunter 307, is early fifteenth century, but its production range overlaps Sloane 3486 and Royal 18 A VI is only broadly dated; no single earliest witness is established

assertion.notes: Hunter's 1400-1449 range overlaps Sloane's 1425-1474 range, while the Royal component is dated only to 1400-1499. The 1400-1449 normalization records the securely supported early-fifteenth-century attestation window without selecting one witness as definitively earliest or excluding a lost earlier exemplar.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English titles, editorial notes, and article prose belong to representation or research layers and do not make the medieval cataloging subject bilingual.

form_statements.description: Continuous instructional and diagnostic medical prose.

assertion.notes: The cataloging subject is the hematoscopy opening of the longer phlebotomy text, not its scarifying and cupping continuation.
Bloodletting is treated as a surgical practice within Guy de Chauliac's Chirurgia magna.
The current BL Royal component record explicitly calls the text a Middle English translation; the precise relation among the three witness versions remains open.

subject_statements.label: Hematoscopy and inspection of drawn blood

assertion.notes: The passage explains what observable blood properties signify about bodily corruption, humoral predominance, moisture, dryness, liver coldness, wind, and related conditions.

summaries.value: The passage instructs a physician to inspect blood during and after bloodletting. Thickness, thinness, heat, cold, congealing, harshness, fat, smell, wateriness, foam, surface evenness, color, brittleness, toughness, and bubbles are interpreted as signs of bodily moisture or dryness, corruption, leprosy, liver coldness, wind, burning, mortification, and the predominance of choler, phlegm, melancholy, or other humours.

CME301 compares the shared opening passage in Sloane MS 3486, f. 147v, and Hunter 307, f. 165v line 14 through f. 166r line 25. It excludes Hunter's continuation on scarifying and cupping beginning at f. 166r line 26 and continuing through f. 166v. Royal MS 18 A VI, ff. 54r-55v, is a third collateral witness to the longer hematoscopy, scarifying, and cupping text; its exact internal end point for the CME301-equivalent passage has not yet been verified.

The XML's sixty P elements each align one Sloane unit with one Hunter unit across a single LB. They are comparative editorial units, not sixty numbered intellectual items, two separate works, or trustworthy counts of manuscript lines. The cataloged unit is one prose work-part with two represented witness versions.

extent.description: One coherent prose hematoscopy passage. The repository representation contains sixty paired P/LB alignment units, each with a Sloane and Hunter reading; those sixty pairs are not independent works or manuscript line counts.

content_parts.label: The "Three Inspections" hematoscopy passage
content_parts.summary: A continuous prose instruction for interpreting the visible and sensory qualities of blood drawn by phlebotomy as diagnostic signs of humoral and bodily conditions.

locators.value: opening subsection within ff. 54r-55v; exact end not yet verified
Hunter text on pp. 114-118 through f. 166r line 25; continuation follows

notes: Complete relative to the cataloged Three Inspections work-part, while explicitly incomplete relative to the longer On Bloodletting text.

related_works.title: On Bloodletting
notes: The cataloging subject is the translated opening hematoscopy passage, not a complete translation of the full treatise.
This work-level relation does not identify the anonymous translator's physical Latin exemplar.

related_works.title: Chirurgia magna
notes: Guy de Chauliac's major Latin surgical compilation was composed in 1363; On Bloodletting belongs to that source context.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, '"Three Inspections" hematoscopy,' General Corpus publication source XML
evidence.locator: HEADER; IDG; TEXT/@LANG; BODY/DIV1/HEAD, OPENER, ARGUMENT, and P elements
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for identifiers, editorial title and MED stencils, Middle English language and prose form, transcriber credits, exact source locators and incipits, comparative structure, and the passage's medical content.
notes: The companion lineage manifest records this source as evidence:repo-xml.

evidence.citation: XPath and boundary inspection of the pinned CME301 XML
evidence.locator: BODY/DIV1: 60 P with one LB each; final P; editorial descendants
evidence.summary: Confirms one prose passage represented by sixty Sloane/Hunter alignment pairs and establishes the representation-specific extent without converting alignment units into work items or manuscript lines.
notes: The companion lineage manifest records this inspection as evidence:repo-structure.

evidence.citation: Esteban-Segura and Soriano-Jiménez, 'Guy de Chauliac's On Bloodletting in Glasgow, University Library, MS Hunter 307 (ff. 165v-166v),' Revista Canaria de Estudios Ingleses 87 (2023): 107-130
evidence.locator: pp. 107-123, especially source discussion, codicology, edition, and conclusion
evidence.summary: Specialist evidence for Guy de Chauliac attribution, Chirurgia magna date, Sloane and Hunter witness dating, Hunter physical and textual boundary, prose medical content, anonymous translator/scribe, semi-diplomatic edition, and Midland/Huntingdonshire localization.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Sloane MS 3486
evidence.summary: Current holding, shelfmark, composite contents, broad date, and access evidence for the Sloane source witness; the exact f. 147v locator comes from the XML and specialist article.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Royal MS 18 A VI
evidence.summary: Current component evidence for a third Middle English Guy de Chauliac hematoscopy, scarifying, and cupping witness with a closely corresponding opening incipit, as well as English origin and fifteenth-century context.

evidence.citation: WebHunter, Hunter 307 Contents & Description and target transcript pages
evidence.summary: Direct digital evidence for Hunter folio mapping, passage boundary, separate continuation, manuscript transcription, and the relation between CME301 and the longer witness text.

open_questions.question: Do the Sloane, Hunter, and Royal witness versions represent one Middle English translation or multiple translations or recensions?
next_steps: Obtain complete target images or transcripts for all three witnesses.
Collate their full hematoscopy passages and the relevant Latin source before assigning a single translation identity.

open_questions.question: When and where was the Middle English translation or translations produced?
next_steps: Keep witness dates and dialect localizations separate while comparing the three versions.
Seek scholarship or textual evidence that distinguishes translation language from later scribal copying.
notes: Hunter's probable Huntingdonshire localization is evidence about that copy's language, not a secure work-level place of translation.

open_questions.question: What exact lines or folio-side boundary in Royal MS 18 A VI correspond to the end of the CME301 Three Inspections passage?
next_steps: Request or inspect Royal MS 18 A VI ff. 54r-55v.
Align its text with the Sloane and Hunter streams and update the content-part locator.

notes: The title is modern and supplied; no manuscript title for this shared passage has been established.
M. Teresa Tavormina and Paul F. Schaffner are recorded as transcribers of the CME representation, not as medieval authors.

The source header's modern keyword 'Hematology' is retained in lineage evidence but not treated as proof that the medieval passage belongs to modern scientific hematology; hematoscopy, phlebotomy, humoral medicine, and diagnosis are the more precise discovery concepts.
Royal MS 18 A VI is a newly verified collateral witness from a current institutional catalog and must not be presented as a manuscript source used to create CME301.
